_meta.note: Session 3 of 7. New file — not originally listed in the four-file data architecture from the design log, added because sparring content needs its own home distinct from article metadata. constitution_articles.json is unaffected. 8 questions this pass, each naming the actual doctrinal test rather than asking for a vote. Contested is offered as a real option, not a trap — some of these are genuinely unsettled.
_meta.schema: Each question: {id, article, test(name of the doctrinal test being applied), scenario, resolution(what a court applying the named test would weigh — NOT a claimed real verdict unless citing an actual case), contestedNote(optional — why Contested is defensible here)}

questions.scenario: A provincial law reserves 10% of civil service posts for women only in departments historically dominated by men. A male applicant challenges it as sex discrimination under Article 25.
questions.resolution: Article 25(3) explicitly permits special provision for the protection of women — the classification test asks whether the distinction has a rational nexus to a legitimate object (correcting historical exclusion) rather than being arbitrary. A quota tied to demonstrated underrepresentation in a specific sector passes; a blanket quota with no evidentiary basis would not.

questions.scenario: A newspaper is banned from publishing a story naming a sitting judge in connection with an ongoing corruption investigation, on the ground that it constitutes contempt of court.
questions.resolution: Article 19 permits restriction on grounds including contempt of court, but the restriction must itself be reasonable — courts weigh whether the ban is proportionate to the harm (undermining judicial proceedings) versus the public interest in reporting on judicial accountability. A blanket pre-publication ban is a much harder sell than post-publication contempt liability for specific false claims.
questions.contestedNote: Genuinely contested — Pakistani courts have not settled a single clear line between prior restraint and after-the-fact contempt liability in cases involving the judiciary itself.

questions.scenario: A special tribunal convicts a defendant based substantially on a confession later alleged to have been obtained under duress, with the defendant given only 48 hours to prepare a defence.
questions.resolution: Article 10-A's due process guarantee weighs adequacy of time to prepare, right to counsel, and reliability of evidence. A confession-heavy conviction with minimal preparation time is a strong due-process violation on the facts as stated — but the outcome depends heavily on what safeguards the tribunal did provide, which a bare hypothetical can't fully settle.

questions.test: Balancing environmental right against economic development
questions.scenario: A provincial government approves a coal power plant near a residential area without a completed environmental impact assessment, citing urgent energy needs.
questions.resolution: Post-9A, this is now a direct textual claim rather than the Shehla Zia interpretive route. Courts weigh the right to a healthy environment against the state's competing policy interest — the missing impact assessment is the strongest single fact, since procedural compliance is usually easier to enforce than substituting the court's own judgment on energy policy.

questions.test: Freedom to profess religion vs. public order restriction
questions.scenario: A religious minority's request to build a new place of worship is denied by local authorities citing 'potential public order concerns' with no specific incident cited.
questions.resolution: Article 20's guarantee is subject to law, public order, and morality — but the restriction itself must be reasoned, not speculative. A denial citing only generalized, non-specific concern is weak under the same reasonableness standard applied to Article 19 restrictions; the burden is on the State to show a concrete basis.
questions.contestedNote: Contested in practice — this is precisely the pattern human rights organizations have flagged repeatedly, but litigated outcomes have been inconsistent.

questions.scenario: The government acquires agricultural land for a highway project and pays compensation based on a valuation from 15 years prior, unadjusted for inflation.
questions.resolution: Article 24 requires law providing for compensation, but doesn't itself fix a valuation method. Courts have generally read 'compensation' as requiring a genuine, current-value assessment rather than a nominal figure — a valuation frozen at a 15-year-old rate is vulnerable, though the precise adequacy threshold is not sharply defined in the text itself.

questions.test: Freedom of movement vs. security restriction
questions.scenario: A district administration imposes a curfew in a specific neighbourhood for one week, citing intelligence about an imminent security threat, with no judicial order authorizing it.
questions.resolution: Article 15 permits restriction by law in the public interest — the live question is whether an administrative curfew order without prior judicial authorization satisfies 'by law' or requires a more specific statutory basis and time-limited review. Short-duration, geographically narrow curfews tied to specific intelligence tend to survive; open-ended ones are harder to defend.

questions.test: Freedom of trade vs. licensing regulation
questions.scenario: A new provincial law requires all street food vendors to obtain a costly annual license with a lengthy application process, effectively pricing out most existing small vendors.
questions.resolution: Article 18 permits regulation by law, including licensing and qualification requirements — but a regulation that functions as a de facto prohibition for an entire class of small operators, rather than a genuine quality/safety qualification, strains the line between 'regulate' and 'restrict out of existence.'
questions.contestedNote: Contested — the line between legitimate regulation and effective prohibition through cost barriers has not been sharply drawn in Pakistani case law for this fact pattern specifically.
